channel.title: Programming on Megha Bose
channel.description: Recent content in Programming on Megha Bose

item.title: How to Think Like a Programmer
item.description: There is a specific moment that almost every programmer remembers. You are staring at a problem - maybe a Sudoku puzzle, maybe a scheduling nightmare at work - and suddenly you stop trying to solve it directly. Instead, you find yourself thinking: what are the pieces here, and how do they connect? You start asking what changes and what stays fixed. You wonder whether this problem resembles something you have seen before.

item.title: Python from Scratch
item.description: Prerequisite: How to Think Like a Programmer In 1991, a Dutch programmer named Guido van Rossum released a language he had been building in his spare time over the Christmas holiday. He named it after Monty Python’s Flying Circus, not the snake. He wanted it to be fun. He wanted it to be readable. He wanted it to feel like executable pseudocode - a language where the code said what it meant.

item.title: Functions, Scope, and Reusable Code
item.description: Prerequisite: Python from Scratch In the early days of programming - the late 1940s and 1950s - there were no functions. Programmers wrote code as a flat sequence of instructions. If you needed to sort something in two different places, you wrote the sorting code in two different places. If there was a bug in that sorting code, you fixed it in two different places - unless you forgot one, which you often did.

item.title: Recursion: When Functions Call Themselves
item.description: Prerequisite: Functions, Scope, and Reusable Code Hold two mirrors facing each other and look inside. You see a hallway of reflections stretching into a vanishing point - each reflection contains an image of itself, slightly smaller, slightly darker, until it disappears into the distance. This is self-reference. The image contains itself. The definition contains itself. It is a structure that should not be able to exist, and yet there it is, perfectly finite, perfectly stable, arising from an infinite regress that stops only because each copy is smaller than the last.

item.title: Object-Oriented Programming
item.description: Prerequisite: Functions, Scope, and Reusable Code In 1967, two Norwegian computer scientists named Ole-Johan Dahl and Kristen Nygaard were working on a problem: how do you simulate the behavior of a shipping port? Ships arrive, dock, unload, wait for cargo, load, depart. Each ship has its own state - its cargo, its position, its schedule. The ships interact with the port, with each other, with the tides. They invented a programming language to solve it.

item.title: C++ Fundamentals
item.description: Every programming language is ultimately a set of rules for turning human intention into machine instructions. C++ has more rules than most, and they are more visible than most. Where Python hides memory allocation, type coercion, and integer width behind convenient defaults, C++ puts these decisions in front of you and asks you to make them explicitly. This is not cruelty - it is the language’s thesis: that control over low-level mechanics, when combined with the right abstractions, produces code that is both fast and composable.

item.title: Design Patterns
item.description: Prerequisite: Object-Oriented Programming In 1977, an architect named Christopher Alexander published A Pattern Language. His subject was buildings and towns, but his method was unusual: he argued that good design arises from recurring solutions to recurring problems, and that these solutions could be named and described formally. A “pattern” in his sense was not a template to be copied - it was a distillation of hard-won wisdom about a recurring situation, available to be applied thoughtfully wherever that situation arose.

item.title: Data Structures: Arrays to Queues
item.description: Prerequisite: Python from Scratch Picture two ways to store a list of a thousand names. In the first, you write every name in a notebook, one after another on consecutive lines. In the second, you write each name on a separate index card, and attach a small sticky note to each card pointing to where the next one is. Both hold a thousand names. But if I ask you to hand me the 500th name, the notebook gets you there in a second - count to line 500.

item.title: Hash Maps and O(1) Lookup
item.description: Prerequisite: Data Structures: Arrays to Queues Imagine you are running the world’s largest library - 10 million books. A patron walks in and asks for a specific title. In the worst version of this library, the books are shelved in no particular order. You start at shelf one and check every book. On a good day, the book is near the front. On a bad day, it is the last one.

item.title: Trees, Heaps, and Hierarchical Data
item.description: Prerequisite: Data Structures: Arrays to Queues Before you ever heard the word “algorithm,” you were already fluent in hierarchical structure. The folder on your desktop contains subfolders, which contain files. Your company has a CEO who reports to a board, with VPs below, then directors, then managers, then engineers. The HTML of the page you are reading right now is a tree: one root element containing children, each of which may have children of their own.

item.title: Debugging and Profiling
item.description: Prerequisite: Writing Clean, Testable Code On September 9, 1947, Grace Hopper’s team at Harvard found that a moth had flown into relay 70 of the Mark II computer and jammed it. They taped the moth into their logbook alongside the note “First actual case of bug being found.” The word “bug” to describe a defect had been in use since at least Thomas Edison, but the moth made it stick.

item.title: Writing Clean, Testable Code
item.description: Prerequisite: Debugging and Profiling You open a file from six months ago. The function is called proc. It takes d, x, and flag. There are no comments. The logic involves a nested loop, three early returns, and a magic number 17 that you no longer remember the significance of. You wrote this. It made perfect sense at the time. This experience is so universal that it has become the canonical argument for clean code: code is written once and read many times.
